narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your background, like your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and LSAT score of 34, which are close to what many successful applicants bring. However, the admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on academic and background factors from past students, estimated a 62% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try—high enough to lead to the regrettable rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3, well below the typical 5.5 average, as it was the strongest signal pushing the model toward expecting a failure. Your Year 1 decile of 3, also lower than the average around 5.7, reinforced this concern, suggesting early and sustained challenges in your academic performance relative to peers. Coming from an upper-middle income family, which is solid but didn't counterbalance these issues as strongly as higher brackets might for others, added another modest push toward the failure prediction. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA actually nudged the prediction slightly toward passing, performing a bit better than it might for some, and your LSAT score provided a small positive lift compared to lower scorers. Planning to attend full-time, like the vast majority of students (over 92%), had only a minor influence and didn't sway things much. Overall, your profile stands out as having lower high school deciles than most admitted students, which overshadowed your other solid metrics and set you apart from those typically predicted to pass. While this decision is tough, especially given the barriers Black women like you often face in these processes, it's not the end of your journey. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your academic deciles through targeted improvement or additional coursework, perhaps retaking the LSAT for a higher score, and highlighting any extracurriculars or experiences that demonstrate resilience. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these key areas—consider community college transfers, prep courses, or gap-year achievements to rebuild your profile. You're capable of great things; keep pushing forward with that strength we see in you.
